coredata.dc:title: Design of porous metal collector via bubble template-assisted electrochemical deposition using numerical simulation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Inoue, Gen
Abe, Shinya
Gao, Ruijing
Park, Kayoung
So, Magnus
Matsukuma, Yosuke
Kimura, Naoki
Tsuge, Yoshifumi
coredata.dc:description: Reduction of resistances in next-generation secondary batteries, which considerably affects battery performance under high-rate conditions, is essential to increase their output density. In particular, lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) require sufficient contact between the collector and the composite electrode layer. However, contact is often disrupted due to the absence of strong bonding. This issue can be redressed using a porous metal to fabricate the collector. In this study, a porous metal structure is theoretically designed and optimized for this purpose. To this end, a numerical model is developed to simulate the formation of the structure via the hydrogen bubble template-assisted electrodeposition method. With the model, the effects of electrodeposition on the process of formation were examined. Via simulation, the performance of the proposed porous metal structure is estimated corresponding to each deposition potential, and the validity of the architecture is confirmed based on experimental results. In addition, a gradient structure is designed to serve as a highly conductive collector during particle packing. The effectiveness of the overall architecture is evaluated via numerical simulation and a multi-step process is proposed to fabricate the gradient structure. Experimental results reveal that the optimal proposed structure exhibits 30% higher conductivity compared to conventional alternatives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical deposition
Bubble template-assisted method
Heterogeneous porous structure
Simulation with electrochemical and mass transport, Effective electrical conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental setup of bubble template-assisted electrodeposition
Effect of acetic acid and the measurement of surface tension
Numerical simulation of bubble template-assisted electrodeposition
Placement of hydrogen bubbles on the Cu surface
Basic equations of hydrogen bubble-assisted electrochemical deposition
Dependence of formation process on voltage and solution concentration

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic image of hydrogen bubble-assisted electrochemical deposition of porous metal and SEM image of the structure.

Experimental observations during the formation of the porous structure: (a) Surface view of Cu after deposition at 0.4 V and 0.08 V, (b) relationship between deposition time and amount of deposited Cu, (c) H2 generation selectivity at each potential, (d) pore size distribution obtained via image processing.

Experimental measurement of the effect of adding acetic acid on the formation of porous structure: (a) schematic image of stability of bubble, (b) surface image of each deposition condition with different acetic acid concentration, (c) relationship between the acetic acid concentration and the average pore size, (d) relationship between surface tension and the average pore size of the porous Cu structure.
Simulation method: (a) schematic image of the multi-block method, (b) boundary conditions in this simulation.

Simulation of the formation of the porous structure corresponding to different solution concentrations (Case 1: 0.1 M CuSO4 + 1.5 M H2SO4, Case 2: 0.2 M CuSO4 + 1.5 M H2SO4): (a) porous structure and (b) reaction distribution at each reaction time, (c) relationship between deposition time and average thickness.

Simulation of the formation of the porous structure at different voltages: (a) porous structure and reaction distribution with 0.2 M CuSO4, (b) pore size distribution, (c) electrical conductivity in the direction perpendicular to the plane of deposition and the surface area, (d) packing result in the structure obtained at 0.4 V, (e) relationship between deposition potential and the amount of GC, resistivity, and resistivity per GC weight.

Simulation of gradient structure, (a) concept of gradient structure, (b) composite structure with and without alteration to the diameter of bubbles, (c) porosity distribution (d) average pore size distribution, (e) pore size distribution in conventional structure (Type A), (f) pore size distribution in gradient structure (Type B) with and without GC, (g) electrical conductivity and the contact surface area.

Experimental observation of gradient structure: (a) protocol of formation of gradient structure, (b) average pore size, (c) picture of dip-coater, (d) SEM image after drying, (e) schematic images of measurement cell, (f) effective electrical resistivity of each structure.
Deposition and electrolyte condition corresponding to each structure.

row.entry: 0.1 M CuSO4+1.5 M H2SO4+0.03 M CH3COOH

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Time means the deposition time in each electrolyte.

head.ce:title: Design of porous metal collector via bubble template-assisted electrochemical deposition using numerical simulation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduction of resistances in next-generation secondary batteries, which considerably affects battery performance under high-rate conditions, is essential to increase their output density. In particular, lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) require sufficient contact between the collector and the composite electrode layer. However, contact is often disrupted due to the absence of strong bonding. This issue can be redressed using a porous metal to fabricate the collector. In this study, a porous metal structure is theoretically designed and optimized for this purpose. To this end, a numerical model is developed to simulate the formation of the structure via the hydrogen bubble template-assisted electrodeposition method. With the model, the effects of electrodeposition on the process of formation were examined. Via simulation, the performance of the proposed porous metal structure is estimated corresponding to each deposition potential, and the validity of the architecture is confirmed based on experimental results. In addition, a gradient structure is designed to serve as a highly conductive collector during particle packing. The effectiveness of the overall architecture is evaluated via numerical simulation and a multi-step process is proposed to fabricate the gradient structure. Experimental results reveal that the optimal proposed structure exhibits 30% higher conductivity compared to conventional alternatives.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Simulation with electrochemical and mass transport, Effective electrical conductivity

ce:def-description.ce:para: effective diffusion coefficient of Cu2+ in electrolyte (m2⋅s−1)
diffusion coefficient of Cu2+ in electrolyte (m2⋅s−1)
current density of Cu electrochemical deposition (A⋅m−2)
exchange current density of Cu electrochemical deposition (A⋅m−2)
difference in pressure between the interior and exterior of a bubble (Pa)
transport number of Cu2+ in electrolyte (-)
porosity or volume fraction of electrolyte phase(-)
bulk electronic conductivity in Cu phase (S⋅m−1)
bulk Cu2+ conductivity in the electrolyte (S⋅m−1)
volume fraction of the Cu phase (-)

ce:section.ce:para: High performance secondary batteries are essential for effective energy storage and utilization in various energy devices and automotive applications. In particular, the power density and durability of lithium-ion batteries (LiBs) require constant improvement because the devices requiring these batteries are getting more sophisticated [1]. This can be achieved by decreasing the internal resistance, which depends on electrochemical reactions, ionic conduction, and electrical conduction. To this end, several innovative materials have been developed, and the effective electrode structure have been designed. Many studies have also been conducted to examine the degradation of cell performance. One factor contributing to the degradation of cell performance is variation in the volume of the active material induced by the insertion of Li. This leads to the generation of an inhomogeneous stress distribution on the electrode layer. Such local variations in stress can be homogenized by utilizing collectors made of porous metals [2–4], which help in dispersing the internal stress because of their solid frame structures. In addition, they exhibit large surface areas, thereby reducing contact resistance between the electrode layer and the collector foil and increasing the effective electrical conductivity. Using porous collector leads to reduce electrical conduction resistance in electrode layer and degradation by charge-discharge cycle. However, in order to increase the cell performance, porous structure of this collector has to be designed by considering effective electrical conductivity, effective contact area and high packing capacity. And the various structural properties, such as porosity, pore size distribution, surface area and connectivity of pore, should be controlled. There are few studies to examine this relationship between porous structure and various properties.

Hydrogen bubble template-assisted electrochemical deposition has been proposed for the production of porous metal substrates [5–8]. Fig. 1 depicts a schematic diagram illustrating the method and a scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of the structure. The variant involving a type of gas bubble dynamic template is considered in this study owing to the several advantages it exhibits compared to other approaches. The porous structure of copper films is generated by a competitive reaction between copper deposition and hydrogen evolution. Hydrogen bubbles generated via the reduction reaction in the deposition bath create a continuous path from the cathode to the electrolyte–air interface during chemical deposition. These bubbles act as dynamic templates during the metal growth process, leading to metal deposition between the bubbles. Further, bubble templates disappear when it is taken out from solution. Thus, the template removal step can be omitted from the process. The porous structure is generated easily via a one-step chemical deposition reaction. Such structures have been extensively studied because of their special structure, high strength, and low weight. Three-dimensional (3D) porous materials are widely used in lightweight construction, energy absorption, sensors, catalytic supports, super capacitors, surgical implants, electrodes of batteries, and fuel cells. The open porous structure of these materials allows rapid transportation of liquid and gas, and provides a large surface area for electrochemical reactions. Therefore, 3D porous materials are particularly suitable as battery electrodes. Recently, dual-functional porous structures have been developed [9]. Moreover, the effect of deposition time on pore structure [10], composition of the electrode material on the formation of the porous structure [11], and pH and EDTA additives on the structure of Ni films as electrocatalysts has been examined [12].

Although 3D porous materials exhibit many desirable characteristics, they suffer from certain problems, e.g., the lack of effective control over the microstructure, pore size and distribution, porosity, wall density of porous structures, as well as the lack of adequate mechanical strength for better utilization of internal surfaces and the fabrication of new materials with similar structures for specific functional devices. In particular, an understanding of the effects of such structures on the reduction of various internal resistances is a prerequisite to their application in battery technology. Numerical simulation is an important method used to optimize such porous structures. In particular, in the case of batteries, the porous electrode theory has been developed, which combines simulation with electrochemical reactions and mass transportation [13]. The simulation method was used to examine the effects of the porous structure on cell performance in various energy devices [14], and thereby to optimize the electrode structure. In this study, the dependence of the selectivity of Cu deposition and H2 evolution on the electrode potential is determined experimentally. Based on this, the simulation model is implemented in combination with electrochemical reactions and mass transportation. The simulation model is used to analyze the formation of the porous structure. Further, an optimal structure for the current collector foil in the secondary battery was designed via simulation, and its effectiveness was evaluated using experiments. The purposes of this study are as follows:
1. To propose new electro deposition method for porous collector.
2. To understand the relationship between porous structure and the electrical properties with actual electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental setup of bubble template-assisted electrodeposition
ce:section.ce:para: Development of a numerical simulation model for electrochemical deposition via the hydrogen bubble template method requires the prior estimation of the selectivity of the electrode reaction corresponding to each potential. This is essential to simulate dynamic structure formation. Thus, the relationship between selectivity and the conditions of electrolysis was estimated experimentally. Additionally, the stability of hydrogen bubbles was estimated by measuring the surface tension of each electrolyte solution.

During electrochemical deposition, a high-purity copper plate (Nilaco, 99.96%) cleaned with dilute hydrochloric acid was used as the cathode (working electrode). The dry clean copper plate was weighed using a balance. Similarly, another copper plate was used as the anode (counter electrode). The distance between the anode and the cathode was maintained at 2 cm during the course of all the experiments. The surface of the working electrode was masked using a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) sheet, and the deposition area size was 0.25 cm2. The primary constituents of the electrodeposition bath were CuSO4 (0.1–0.6 M) and H2SO4 (1.5 M, 50 mL). The deposition power was supplied by a direct power supply (A&D AD8735). The load conditions, e.g., current, voltage, and resistance, were controlled and measured using a potentiostat and impedance meter (KIKUSUI, KFM 2005) and recorded in a personal computer. A stable, high current density (1–6 A/cm2) was applied to achieve electrochemical copper deposition at room temperature. Following deposition, the sample material was dried well, and its thickness and weight were measured to ascertain the deposition rate under each condition. To analyze the structure of the generated porous copper plate, photographs with different degrees of magnification were captured using a digital microscope (KEYENCE, VHX-200) and a scanning electron microscope (SHIMADZU, SS-550). Image processing techniques were used to estimate the feature parameters of the porous structure from the captured images. After binarization and noise removal, mean pore size and pore size distribution were ascertained automatically. The selectivity was calculated based on the current efficiency of each reaction, which was calculated based on the difference in weight of the copper electrode. The method to obtain the selectivity from experimental results is shown in the explanation of Fig.2.

Fig. 2 depicts the result of the electrodeposition process. Fig. 2(a) presents the surface of the copper electrode following deposition under potentials of 0.4 V and 0.08 V. The electrolyte was mixture solution of 0.2 M CuSO4 and 1.5 M H2SO4. Hydrogen bubbles were generated by maintaining the electrode potential above 0.19 V, which is the hydrogen generation voltage for a copper electrode. As is evident from the figure, no void spaces were created at 0.08 V, whereas the copper surface exhibited void spaces at 0.4 V. Fig. 2(b) illustrates the relationship between deposition time and the deposited amount of copper. The theoretical value refers to the estimated result in the absence of hydrogen bubble generation (i.e., assuming Cu deposition current efficiency to be 100%.). The graph demonstrates that, at 0.08 V, the experimental result is almost identical to the theoretical value. This confirms that hydrogen bubbles were not generated in this case. Based on this result, the H2 generation selectivity at each potential was obtained, as recorded in Fig. 2(c). The H2 generation selectivity was observed to share a linear relationship with potential above the 0.19 V point—this equation was included in our simulation model. In addition, Fig. 2(d) depicts the pore size distribution obtained via image processing. The average pore size was approximately 30 μm—this pore size distribution was used to simulate hydrogen bubble generation on the surface of the Cu electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of acetic acid and the measurement of surface tension
ce:section.ce:para:
Shin et al. reported that adding acetic acid decreases pore size in porous structures because the acetic acid acts as a bubble stabilizer [5]. In this study, addition of acetic acid is used as one of the methods to control the structure of the porous Cu electrode. However, the effect of acetic acid on the fluid dynamics behavior, such as detachment, floating and coalescence of bubbles, has not been investigated comprehensively. Thus, we considered the effect from the perspective of the internal pressure of a bubble. We assumed that the stability of a bubble depends on the surface tension of its boundary because its internal pressure can be expressed by the Young–Laplace equation, as follows:
(1)
Δ
p = 2 γ / r
where Δp denotes the difference in pressure between the gas and the liquid, γ denotes the surface tension, and r denotes the bubble radius. Fig. 3 (a) depicts a schematic image of this mechanism. If the internal pressure of a bubble remains constant, the pore size is directly proportional to the surface tension. Thus, we measured the surface tension of each electrolyte with varying concentrations of acetic acid using the Wilhelmy method (KYOWA, DY-300). The meniscus gas–liquid interface was formed on the surface of the Pt plate, which was oriented perpendicularly with respect to liquid surface, and the force of the Pt plate was measured. Fig. 3(b) depicts the surface of the copper electrode under each deposition condition and acetic acid concentration. In the case without acetic acid, the average pore size was 28 μm; when the acetic acid concentration was increased to 0.1 mol/L, it became 16 μm. Fig. 3(c) illustrates the relationship between the acetic acid concentration and the average pore size. As is evident from the figure, the average pore size was observed to decrease when the concentration of acetic acid was increased. Fig. 3(d) illustrates the relationship between surface tension and the average pore size of the porous Cu structure. It was found that the average pore size increased linearly with an increase in surface tension. It is expected that the minimum bubble size strongly depends on the roughness of the Cu surface and that the internal pressure of a bubble depends on the rate of hydrogen evolution. Accordingly, Eq. (1) is not satisfied under all conditions. However, within the range of acetic acid concentration depicted in Fig. 3(d), the relationship between surface tension and the average pore size was almost linear. Thus, the equation for pore size with respect to the acetic acid concentration was obtained based on this result.

Andrea Angulo et al. explain the theoretical model of bubble evolution in their review article with various studies [15]. And they also derive the critical radius for diffusive stability of a bubbles in a supersaturated solution (not growing or shrinking) with Young- Laplace equation. In Fig.3 (d) we confirm that the pore size increases as surface tension increases. As the porous structure is generated with hydrogen bubbles as template, this relationship can be expressed by Young- Laplace equation. However, the average pore size is not perfectly proportional in surface tension. In other words, this predicted line doesn't pass the origin of coordinates. Because the pore structure is formed with many bubbles. Thus, as the pore diameter of porous metal structure is not the same as the single bubble diameter. In this study, we use this equation in Fig.3 (d) to reproduce pore structure in each solution condition.

Additionally, we should consider the effect of adding acetic acid on porous structure from the viewpoint of electrochemical reaction activity. In the case of adding acetic acid, limiting current of H2 bubble generation reaction increase because the mass transport rate of hydrogen ion increases. As the nucleation and detachment of bubbles are occurred rapidly, the growth of bubble size is limited. Thus, the porous metal structure has high porosity and small pore diameter. This is one phenomenon of the predicted mechanism. In order to check the validity of this mechanism, in our future study, we need to measure the resistance of mass transport and electrochemical reaction with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical simulation of bubble template-assisted electrodeposition
Placement of hydrogen bubbles on the Cu surface
ce:section.ce:para: It is difficult to numerically simulate dynamic behavior of hydrogen bubbles during electrodeposition as the attachment, detachment, and coalescence of bubbles have to be taken into account. However, images of the surface of the porous structure also reveal stable bubble templates, demonstrating that static bubbles can exist on the surface of the copper plate. In this study, this placement of hydrogen bubbles on the Cu surface was simulated. The optimal diameter of the bubbles and the number of bubbles were determined based on the pore size distribution estimated via image processing and the current efficiency of hydrogen evolution, respectively. The number of bubbles generated in unit time was determined by the hydrogen reaction rate based on total current and selectivity. Following these steps, hydrogen bubbles were placed on the Cu surface randomly avoiding overlap between pairs of bubbles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic equations of hydrogen bubble-assisted electrochemical deposition
ce:section.ce:para:
A simulation model with electrochemical reactions and mass transport was developed by using the porous electrode theory which is often used for battery simulation [13]. The equations of the reaction and mass transport that are part of hydrogen bubble-assisted electrochemical deposition are as follows:

V
where ε denotes the local volume fraction of the electrolyte,
C
C
u
2 +
denotes Cu2+ concentration,
D
C
u
2 +
e ff
denotes the effective diffusion coefficient of Cu2+,
i
C
u
2 +
denotes the current density of the Cu deposition reaction,
i
C

0
denotes its exchange current density,
t
C
u
2 +
0
denotes the number of Cu2+ transports in the electrolyte, α denotes the transfer coefficient of the electrode reaction, η denotes the over-voltage,
σ
C
u
2 +
e ff
and
σ
e −
e ff
denote the effective conductivities of Cu2+ and an electron, respectively. Further,
ϕ
C
u
2 +
and
ϕ
e −
denote the local potentials of Cu2+ and an electron, F denotes Faraday constant, R denotes gas constant, T denotes temperature,
S Cu
denotes the reaction rate during the solid phase,
M Cu
denotes molecular weight,
ρ Cu
denotes the density of Cu, and V denotes the local volume. The Cu2+ concentration distribution was calculated using Eq. (2) with the diffusion and deposition terms. The local current density was calculated using the Bulter–Volmer equation and Eq. (3), and the Cu2+ potential and the electro-potential are calculated using Eq. (4) and (5). Eq. (6) depicts the dynamic change in the volume fraction of the electrolyte. In the simulation model, the following assumptions were adopted:
•
The chemical and physical properties of deposited Cu are identical to those of the original Cu plate.
•
Change in solution concentration induced by deposition is ignored.
•
Heat loss due to overvoltage is ignored.

In this study it is assumed that there is little proton concentration distribution and proton potential distribution in electrolyte because of the proton concentration is larger than that of Cu ion. Thus, the proton transfer in electrolyte is ignored. With this assumption, the generation of hydrogen bubbles does not depend on the proton transfer distance in porous structure. About the simulation of electro deposition system, various researchers have already proposed detailed simulation models, such as fluids and sediments. And in order to simulate detailed interface phenomena in electro deposition system, we should combine more detailed models in this simulation to understand the multi-physics phenomena in nano-scale at the electrochemical interface, such as electrostatic migration and fluid flow [16–18]. However, in this study we focus on the electro deposition for porous metal with hydrogen template method. And the effect of porous structure on secondary battery performance is considered. Since this is the very complex phenomena, we use various assumptions and some phenomena are ignored. In this study, we use the simple models in order to understand the effect of electro deposition condition on complex porous structures for a secondary battery performance.

A 3D simulation method was considered in this study to simulate the formation of a heterogeneous porous structure. Several two-phase flow models, e.g., volume of fluid and lattice Boltzmann methods, have been proposed [19]. However, simulating them over large volumes with the concurrent formation of a solid phase is difficult. In this study, we used the multi-block method, which consists of a coarse-grained block with three phases (i.e., solid, bubbles, and electrolyte) [20–22]. The 3D calculation mesh used in this simulation was converted to a block structure, where the bubble diameter was determined using the coarse-grained method and the local transport coefficients, e.g., the effective diffusion coefficient and the effective conductivity, were calculated based on the local volume fraction and local tortuosity of each phase at the interfaces between the blocks. Fig. 4 (a) depicts a schematic image of this method. Conventionally, the effective ionic conductivity and the effective diffusion coefficient in porous media are calculated using the Bruggeman equation. Although this equation can be used in static porous media, which consist of particle packing structures, local transport properties cannot be expressed using it because the local volume fraction is in dynamic flux in this case. Thus, in this paper, the tortuosity of τ and τ Cu corresponding to electrolyte phase and Cu solid were calculated with the random walk method [23], and the effective ionic conductivity and the effective diffusion coefficient were calculated with the following equations [23–25].
(7)
D
C
u
2 +
e ff
=
D
C
u
2 +
×
ε
/ τ
(8)
σ
C
u
2 +
e ff
=
σ
C
u
2 +
×
ε
/ τ
(9)
σ
e
−
e ff
=
σ
e
−
×
φ Cu
/
τ Cu

Typically, the tortuosity is calculated from the ratio of the mean square length in free space to the mean square length in porous media with the same step number. In our previous study, to calculate the anisotropic tortuosity, the start and end planes of a random walk were fixed in the same direction, and the tortuosity was calculated from the mean square of the number of steps between these two planes. Thus, we add the following sentences to the manuscript. In general, effective conductivity of porous media is included the overall structural properties (porosity and tortuosity) in porous media. And this value is assumed uniform and constant in porous media locally. However, in actual heterogeneous structure, the local solid phase structure which is conduction path of electron is not homogeneous. Thus, in order to simulate the formation of heterogeneous porous structure with electro deposition, local effective conductivity is calculated with the volume fraction and the tortuosity of solid phase in each calculation mesh. On the other hand, in the case of Cu ion conduction in electrolyte, this approach is the same. The local effective ion conductivity is calculated with the volume fraction and the tortuosity of electrolyte phase in each calculation mesh. In conventional and simple case, BV equation is often used as boundary condition at the interface between solid phase and electrolyte phase. However, this simulation model is different conventional cases. In this study, the generation of porous metal structure is simulated. The multi-block method, which consists of coarse-grained blocks with three phases (i.e., solid, bubbles, and electrolyte), is used. Accordingly, each block calculated in this study has solid, liquid and gas phase. The boundary interface between electrolyte and solid was not calculated clearly. And BV equation is needed to connect the electron potential and ion potential in each block. Thus, the BV equation is one of the governing equation.

The 3D staggered mesh was utilized in a Cartesian coordinate system to simulate vectors (conduction and diffusion) and scalar (concentration and potential) quantities. The implicit Euler method was used for time discretization and the finite difference method was used for space discretization. The successive over-relaxation method was used to solve the various Poisson-type equations for quantities such as concentration and potential. Table 1 records the conditions and systems used during calculation.

Fig. 4(b) illustrates the boundary condition used in the simulation. As the lower boundary is the flat Cu plate, electron potential is constant value (input condition), the gradients of ion potential and concentration are zero. The lateral boundary is given by the wall condition, thus, the gradients of all potential and concentration are zero. The upper boundary is defined based on uniform electrolyte concentration. Accordingly, the gradient of electron potential is zero, ion potential and concentration are constant value (input condition). At the solid–liquid interface, the potentials of Cu2+ and electrons satisfied the Eq. (3), (4) and (5) of the electrochemical reaction. In this simulation, as the initial condition with no electrochemical reaction, the local concentration and local potential are constant and uniform. The volume fraction of electrolyte phase is 1.

During the calculation of local current density, the effective surface area corresponding to each time step was included in the estimation of the apparent current density in each block. Eqs. (2)–(6) were calculated simultaneously with the electro-neutrality condition, and the hydrogen bubbles-assisted formation of the porous Cu structure was simulated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dependence of formation process on voltage and solution concentration
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 depicts the simulated result of the formation of the porous structure corresponding to different solution concentrations (Case 1: 0.1 M CuSO4 + 1.5 M H2SO4, Case 2: 0.2 M CuSO4 + 1.5 M H2SO4). Figs. 5(a) and (b) depict the porous structures and the reaction distributions at different points of the reaction. The deposition voltage was fixed to 0.4 V. The results demonstrate that the deposition thickness, the porous morphology, and the local reaction rate depend on the solution concentration. In addition, high deposition was observed to be concentrated on the upper area of the porous structure because of the dominance of Cu2+ transport in the electrolyte. Fig. 5(c) depicts the relationship between the simulated and empirical estimates of the deposition time and the average thickness. The thickness was empirically estimated based on height measurements using a digital microscope—these results are included within the supplementary data (Fig. S1). As is evident from Fig. 5(c), the experimental and calculated results were almost identical corresponding to both electrolyte compositions. This confirmed the validity of the proposed simulation model. The height distribution obtained by laser microscope is shown in the section of supplemental data. In this study, the surface roughness and waviness of the samples have not been obtained experimentally. These properties are generally obtained for smooth substrate which is not porous media. In this study, each samples have large pores which have 20 μm diameter, the porosity is more than 0.5, and the thickness is approximately 100 μm. The internal porous structure is dominant compared with the surface condition. Accordingly, the surface roughness and waviness of the samples are not used as important data. In our future study, we will try to evaluate the relationship between the electro deposition condition and the surface structure with new high precision measurement technology.

Next, the effect of deposition voltage on the process of formation was examined. Fig. 6 depicts the simulated results of the formation of the porous structure at different voltages. Fig. 6(a) shows the porous structure and reaction distribution in each voltage condition. The electrolyte is 0.2 M of CuSO4. Fig. 6(b) depicts the pore size distribution in each case. Under high voltage, the porosity increases and the pore size distribution becomes wider because of the high selectivity of hydrogen evolution. Fig. 6(c) illustrates the electrical conductivity in the direction perpendicular to the plane and the surface area of the porous structure at each deposition potential estimated via simulation. It is observed that increasing the voltage decreases the surface area as well as the electrical conductivity because of the increasing abundance of void spaces in the structure. To serve as an efficient collector substrate in a battery, the porous structure is required to have a large surface area and a large number of void spaces, as these ensure effective contact condition and high capacity. However, excessive porosity degrades electrical conductivity. Thus, these properties have a trade-off relationship. In order to identify the most effective structure to serve as a collector substrate, we simulated the composite structure virtually by packing anode active material. In actual secondary battery, there is the composite electrode layer consisting of active material, void space, binder, and collector substrate. Active material is the most important material for battery. As the cathode active material, lithium cobalt oxide (LCO), NMC (Ni, Mn and Co alloy with lithium) are often used. As the anode active material, graphite, Sn and Si are often used. In this study, we used grassy carbon (GC) as the active material particle. To improve the cell performance, the volume energy density (mAh/L) has to be increased. Thus, active material particles should be filled well in void space of porous collector.

Fig. 6(d) depicts the packing result in the structure obtained at 0.4 V. The parameters of GC, e.g., a particle diameter of 15 μm, a density of 2.0 g/cc, and an electrical conductivity of 4.5 × 10−5 S/m was used during the simulation. During the actual fabrication process, the porous collector was coated with a wet slurry comprising a solvent and an active material such that the void spaces were filled in by the particles. The isolated pores and void spaces whose diameters are smaller than those of the particles of the active materials are unfilled by particles. During the simulation, particles were packed into void spaces on the upper surface of the porous structure that were larger than the particles of active material in terms of diameter [26]. Fig. 6(e) depicts the relationships between the deposition potential and the amount of GC (g/cc), the resistivity (nW⋅m), and the resistivity per GC weight (mW⋅m/g). The resistivity was calculated based on the permeation current at constant differential potential. As is evident from the figure, increasing the voltage increased the amount of GC as the volume of void spaces also increased. However, the electrical resistivity decreased with an increase in voltage and the minimum resistivity per GC weight corresponded to a deposition voltage of 0.3 V. These results demonstrate that the optimal deposition voltage can be determined by minimizing the electrical resistance per unit amount of active material using the proposed numerical simulation model.

In order to increase the volume of active material packed into void spaces, effective pores have to be generated. Further, connectivity of pores from the upper surface to the lower surface should be ensured through efficient design and the number of isolated pores should be reduced. In this study, we utilized the gradient structure to achieve the aforementioned objectives [27]. Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the concept of the proposed structure. It was expected that the number of particles could be increased by enlarging the pores from the upper to the lower surfaces, while simultaneously preserving high electrical conductivity and high porosity. The simulated gradient structure was generated by varying the diameters of hydrogen bubbles in the direction perpendicular to the plane of deposition. In order to simulate the gradient porous structure, the diameter of bubble is changed linearly from 10 μm to 40 μm in the direction of through-plane. Accordingly, porosity is high at the surface of porous Cu. Fig. 7(b) depicts the composite structure before and after altering the diameter of the bubbles. Throughout the simulation, the deposition cell voltage was set to 0.4 V. In the figure, Type A denotes the conventional structure and Type B denotes the gradient structure, Figs. 7(c) and (d) depict the porosity distribution and the average pore size distribution, respectively. The effect of the morphology on the electrical conductivity and packing density was examined based on these data. Figs. 7(e) and (f) depict the pore size distribution in the presence and absence of GC, respectively, in the porous structures. The amount of GC was observed to be 0.269 g/cc and 0.408 g/cc in Type A and Type B structures, respectively. In the case of Type A, large pores, e.g., those with a 50 μm diameter, remained unfilled following packing with GC, indicating that the particle packing in void spaces was not sufficient. Conversely, in Type B structure, particles were packed into large pores satisfactorily. Thus, the pore sizes decreased following GC packing. Fig. 7(g) depicts the electrical conductivity and the contact surface area of each structure. The effective electrical conductivity of Type B structure was 27% higher than that of Type A structure. Similarly, the effective surface area of Type B structure was 8% higher than that of Type A variant. Thus, the simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the gradient structure.

Next, the proposed structure was evaluated empirically. In order to fabricate the gradient porous structure, acetic acid was added to control the diameter of hydrogen bubbles. Table 2 lists the deposition condition and electrolytic composition of each structure. Fig. 8 (a) illustrates the protocol of this method. In order to construct the gradient structure, three kinds of electrolyte solution with different acetic acid concentrations were used. Increasing the acetic acid concentration in each step generated small pores toward the lower surface and large pores near the upper surface. In this paper, three types of structures were fabricated—without acetic acid (Normal), with constant acetic acid concentration (Type A), and with variable acetic acid concentration (Type B)—and their characteristics were compared. In all cases, deposition current is the same (3A). Each deposition time is 30 second. Thus, in the case of Type B, the deposition time is 10 second in each step. After getting porous structure, pore size is measured by digital microscope and image processing of each type and time. Fig. 8(b) depicts the average pore size corresponding to different deposition times. The figure reveals that pore size distributions of Type A and Type B structures in the direction perpendicular to the deposition plane became the gradient distribution. Following the fabrication and drying of the porous structures, GC particles were used to fill in the void spaces using a dip-coater. This system is depicted in Fig. 8(c). The porous substrate was dipped in the GC dispersion solution and the small pores were filled in with GC particles via the capillary force. Fig. 8(d) depicts SEM images of the dried structures. The images confirmed that the void spaces were filled in with the GC particles. Next, the electrical resistance was measured via the four-terminal sensing method [28]. The schematic images of the measurement cell are depicted in Fig. 8(e). Porous Cu plate with GC is sandwiched between gold-plating collectors. Silicon film is used as frame structure to keep the thickness of Porous Cu. These plates are held by 8 bolts. And the Tightening torque is 1 Nm. Fig. 8(f) records the effective electrical resistivity of each structure. The electrical resistivity was observed to decrease with the addition of acetic acid. Further, Type B structure, fabricated using the proposed three-step protocol with different acetic acid concentrations exhibited 32% lower resistivity that Type A structure. These experimental observations corroborated the effectiveness of the gradient structure. The gradient structure of Type A structure can be attributed to bubble coalescence. At the interface of the flat base plate, each bubble was small and separated from the others. However, they began to coalesce as they rose toward the upper surface and increased in size. As a result, the pore size increased along a gradient toward the upper surface. Further, the use of acetic acid further increased bubble coalescence as it created more small bubbles. Thus, statistical incorporation of the effect of bubble coalescence into the simulation model and combining it with a model for the electrodeposition reaction and mass transport is expected to improve its performance. In this study, the main purpose is to obtain the optimal porous metal structure with high electrical conductivity and high electrode capacity for secondary battery. In addition, the contact surface area is important for the conduction of electron from collector foil to each active material. These properties are evaluated as composite electrode layer consisting of porous metal structure and packed active material particles. Active material particles have different average diameter and the particle size distribution. This depends on the particle packing condition to void space of porous metal. Thus, it is not possible to decide the optimal structure by considering only porosity and pore size. However, in our future study, we should establish the design theory or porous structure in each case with different active material.Eqn 7– 9

In this paper, a numerical simulation model was developed to analyze hydrogen bubble template-assisted formation of porous metal structures using a combination of electrochemical deposition reaction and mass transport. In addition, some chemical and structural properties of the fabricated surface were measured experimentally to optimize the simulation model. The following results were obtained.
•
The selectivity of Cu deposition and hydrogen evolution were measured at different potentials and modeled using the simulation model.
•
The thickness of the simulated porous structure was almost identical to that of the actual structure corresponding to each voltage and each solution composition, thereby confirming the validity of the simulation model.
•
The relationships between structure and electrical resistivity were estimated both in the presence and the absence of particle packing using an active material.
•
A model equation was developed to capture the effect of acetic acid on pore size on the basis of the Young–Laplace equation.
•
To utilize the proposed structure as a collector, a gradient structure was proposed. The effectiveness of this structure in reducing electrical resistivity was confirmed via simulation.
•
Finally, the gradient structure was fabricated using a three-step method. It was observed to increase electrical conductivity by 30% compared to the conventional structure.

The numerical simulation model developed in this study adopts certain assumptions regarding the effects of bubble coalescence, heat loss, bubble shape, detachment, nano-scale surface structures, and fluid dynamics [29, 30]. We intend to consider these nuances in future studies. The proposed simulation model can also be applied to fabricate other porous structure via electrochemical deposition [31, 32]. Empirical evaluation of the model in such cases and the optimization of various structures in different devices will be carried out in future studies as well.
Fig. S1. Experimental measurement of the height distribution of the porous structure.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by JSPS KAKENHI Grant-in-Aid for Scientific Research (B), “Creation of innovative process of porous structure by applying electric potential", (grant number 21H01695).
